en.extract:
The chukar partridge (Alectoris chukar), or simply chukar, is a Palearctic upland gamebird in the pheasant family Phasianidae. It has been considered to form a superspecies complex along with the rock partridge, Philby's partridge and Przevalski's partridge and treated in the past as conspecific particularly with the first. This partridge has well-marked black and white bars on the flanks and a black band running from the forehead across the eye down the head to form a necklace that encloses a white throat. Native to Asia, the species has been introduced into many other places and feral populations have established themselves in parts of North America, Malta and New Zealand. This bird can be found in parts of Middle East and temperate Asia.


Description

The chukar is a rotund 32–35 cm (13–14 in) long partridge, with a light brown back, grey breast, and buff belly. The shades vary across the various populations. The face is white with a black gorget. It has rufous-streaked flanks, red legs and coral red bill. Sexes are similar, the female slightly smaller in size and lacking the spur. The tail has 14 feathers, the third primary is the longest while the first is level with the fifth and sixth primaries.
It is very similar to the rock partridge (Alectoris graeca) with which it has been lumped in the past but is browner on the back and has a yellowish tinge to the foreneck. The sharply defined gorget distinguishes this species from the red-legged partridge which has the black collar breaking into dark streaks near the breast. Their song is a noisy chuck-chuck-chukar-chukar from which the name is derived. The Barbary partridge (Alectoris barbara) has a reddish-brown rather than black collar with a grey throat and face with a chestnut crown.
Other common names of this bird include chukker (chuker or chukor), Indian chukar and keklik.


Distribution and habitat

This partridge has its native range in Asia, including Israel, Jordan, Palestine, Lebanon, Syria, United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Turkey, Kurdistan, Iran, Afghanistan, Tajikistan, Pakistan and India, along the inner ranges of the western Himalayas to Nepal, as well as southeasternmost Europe in Bulgaria and Greece. Further west in southeastern Europe it is replaced by the rock partridge, Alectoris graeca. It barely ranges into Africa on the Sinai Peninsula. The habitat in the native range is rocky open hillsides with grass or scattered scrub or cultivation. In Israel and Jordan it is found at low altitudes, starting at 400 m (1,300 ft) below sea level in the Dead Sea area, whereas in the more eastern areas it is mainly found at an altitude of 2,000 to 4,000 m (6,600 to 13,100 ft) except in Pakistan, where it occurs at 600 m (2,000 ft). They are not found in areas of high humidity or rainfall.
It has been introduced widely as a game bird, and feral populations have become established in the United States (Rocky Mountains, Great Basin, high desert areas of California), Canada, Chile, Argentina, New Zealand and Hawaii. Initial introductions into the US were from the nominate populations collected from Afghanistan and Nepal. The birds are hunted across the dry, rocky Columbia Basin, especially in the vicinity of the Snake River in Washington and Oregon. It has also been introduced to New South Wales in Australia but breeding populations have not persisted and are probably extinct. A small population exists on Robben Island in South Africa since it was introduced there in 1964.
The chukar readily interbreeds with the red-legged partridge (Alectoris rufa), and the practice of breeding and releasing captive-bred hybrids has been banned in various countries including the United Kingdom, as it is a threat to wild populations.


Systematics and taxonomy

The chukar partridge is part of a confusing group of "red-legged partridges". Several plumage variations within the widespread distribution of the chukar partridge have been described and designated as subspecies. In the past the chukar group was included with the rock partridge (also known as the Greek partridge). The species from Turkey and farther east was subsequently separated from A. graeca of Greece and Bulgaria and western Europe.


Subspecies
The chukar has 14 recognized subspecies:

A. c. chukar (nominate species) (J. E. Gray, 1830) - eastern Afghanistan to eastern Nepal
A. c. cypriotes (Hartert, 1917) - southeastern Bulgaria to southern Syria, Crete, Rhodes and Cyprus
A. c. dzungarica (Sushkin, 1927) – northwestern Mongolia to Russian Altai and eastern Tibet
A. c. falki (Hartert, 1917) – north central Afghanistan to Pamir Mountains and western China
A. c. kleini (Hartert, 1925) – northern Greece through Bulgaria and north Turkey to the Caucasus
A. c. koroviakovi (Zarudny, 1914) – eastern Iran to Pakistan
A. c. kurdestanica (Meinertzhagen, 1923) – Caucasus Mountains to Iran
A. c. pallescens (Hume, 1873)  – northeastern Afghanistan to Ladakh and western Tibet
A. c. pallida (Hume, 1873) – northwestern China
A. c. potanini (Sushkin, 1927) – western Mongolia
A. c. pubescens (R. Swinhoe, 1871) – inner Mongolia to northwestern Sichuan and eastern Qinghai
A. c. sinaica (Bonaparte, 1858) – northern Syrian Desert to Sinai Peninsula
A. c. subpallida (Zarudny, 1914) – Kyzyl Kum and Kara Kum Mountains (Tajikistan)
A. c. werae (Zarudny and Loudon, 1904) – eastern Iraq and southwestern Iran


Population and status

This species is relatively unaffected by hunting or loss of habitat. Its numbers are largely affected by weather patterns during the breeding season. The release of captive stock in some parts of southern Europe can threaten native populations of rock partridge and red-legged partridge with which they may hybridize.
British sportsmen in India considered the chukar as good sport although they were not considered to be particularly good in flavour. Their fast flight and ability to fly some distance after being shot made recovery of the birds difficult without retriever dogs. During cold winters, when the higher areas are covered in snow, people in Kashmir have been known to use a technique to tire the birds out to catch them.


Behaviour and ecology
In the non-breeding season, chukar partridge are found in small coveys of 10 or more (up to 50) birds. In summer, chukars form pairs to breed. During this time, the cocks are very pugnacious in their calling and fighting. During winter they descend into the valleys and feed in fields. They call frequently during the day and especially in the mornings and evenings. The call is loud and includes loud repeated chuck notes and sometimes duetting chuker notes. Several calls varying with context have been noted. The most common call is a "rallying call" which when played back elicits a response from birds and has been used in surveys, although the method is not very reliable. When disturbed, it prefers to run rather than fly, but if necessary it flies a short distance often down a slope on rounded wings, calling immediately after alighting. In Utah, birds were found to forage in an area of about 2.6 km2 (1.0 sq mi) and travel up to 4.8 km (3.0 mi) to obtain water during the dry season. The home range was found to be even smaller in Idaho.

The breeding season is summer. Males perform tidbitting displays, a form of courtship feeding where the male pecks at food and a female may visit to peck in response. The males may chase females with head lowered, wing lowered and neck fluffed. The male may also perform a high step stiff walk while making a special call. The female may then crouch in acceptance and the male mounts to copulate, while grasping the nape of the female. Males are monogamous. The nest is a scantily lined ground scrape, though occasionally a compact pad is created with a depression in the centre. Generally, the nests are sheltered by ferns and small bushes, or placed in a dip or rocky hillside under an overhanging rock. About 7 to 14 eggs are laid. The eggs hatch in about 23–25 days. In captivity they can lay an egg each day during the breeding season if eggs are collected daily. Chicks join their parents in foraging and will soon join the chicks of other members of the covey.As young chukars grow, and before flying for the first time, they utilize wing-assisted incline running as a transition to adult flight. This behaviour is found in several bird species, but has been extensively studied in chukar chicks, as a model to explain the evolution of avian flight.

Chukar will take a wide variety of seeds and some insects as food. It also ingests grit. In Kashmir, the seeds of a species of Eragrostis was particularly dominant in their diet while those in the US favoured Bromus tectorum. Birds feeding on succulent vegetation make up for their water needs but visit open water in summer.
Chukar roost on rocky slopes or under shrubs. In the winter, birds in the US selected protected niches or caves. A group may roost in a tight circle with their heads pointed outwards to conserve heat and keep a look out for predators.
Chukar are sometimes preyed on by golden eagles.
Birds in captivity can die from Mycoplasma infection and outbreaks of other diseases such as erysipelas.


In culture
The name is onomatopoeic and mentions of chakor in Sanskrit, from northern Indian date back to the Markandeya Purana (c. 250–500 AD). In North Indian and Pakistani culture, as well as in Hindu mythology, the chukar (referred as Chakor) sometimes symbolizes intense, and often unrequited, love. It is said to be in love with the moon and gazes at it constantly. Because of their pugnacious behaviour during the breeding season they are kept in some areas as fighting birds.


Gallery


References


External links

"Alectoris chukar". Integrated Taxonomic Information System. Retrieved 24 February 2009.
Chukar – Alectoris chukar – USGS Patuxent Bird Identification InfoCenter
eNature.com – Chukar
"Chukar media". Internet Bird Collection.
Chukar photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)

The '''chukar partridge''' ('''''Alectoris chukar'''''), or simply '''chukar''', is a [[Palearctic]] upland [[Upland game|gamebird]] in the [[pheasant]] family [[Phasianidae]]. It has been considered to form a [[superspecies complex]] along with the [[rock partridge]], [[Philby's partridge]] and [[Przevalski's partridge]] and treated in the past as conspecific particularly with the first. This [[partridge]] has well-marked black and white bars on the flanks and a black band running from the forehead across the eye down the head to form a necklace that encloses a white throat. Native to Asia, the species has been introduced into many other places and feral populations have established themselves in parts of North America, Malta and New Zealand. This bird can be found in parts of Middle East and temperate Asia.

==Description==
[[File:Chukar Partridge from Ladakh.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Chukar at [[Chang La]], [[Ladakh]], India.]]
[[File:Alectoris chukar hm.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Illustration from [[Allan Octavian Hume|Hume]] and Marshall's ''Game Birds of India, Burma and Ceylon'']]

The chukar is a rotund {{Convert|32|-|35|cm|abbr = on}} long partridge, with a light brown back, grey breast, and buff belly. The shades vary across the various populations. The face is white with a black [[gorget (birds)|gorget]]. It has [[Wikt:rufous|rufous]]-streaked flanks, red legs and coral red bill. Sexes are similar, the female slightly smaller in size and lacking the spur.<ref name="pcr">{{cite book |last=Rasmussen |first=Pamela C. |author-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen |title=Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide |last2=Anderton |first2=John C. |publisher=Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions |year=2005 |isbn=84-87334-66-0 |volume=2 |page=120}}</ref> The tail has 14 feathers, the third primary is the longest while the first is level with the fifth and sixth primaries.<ref name="fbi">{{cite book |author=Blanford |first=William Thomas |author-link=William Thomas Blanford |url=https://archive.org/stream/birdsindia04oaterich#page/131/mode/1up/ |title=Fauna of British India. Birds. Volume 4 |publisher=Taylor and Francis, London |year=1898 |pages=131–132}}</ref>

It is very similar to the [[rock partridge]] (''Alectoris graeca'') with which it has been lumped in the past<ref name=watson>{{cite journal|author=Watson GE|title=Three sibling species of ''Alectoris'' Partridge|journal= Ibis| volume=104|issue=3| pages=353–367| year= 1962| doi= 10.1111/j.1474-919X.1962.tb08663.x}}</ref> but is browner on the back and has a yellowish tinge to the foreneck. The sharply defined gorget distinguishes this species from the [[red-legged partridge]] which has the black collar breaking into dark streaks near the breast. Their song is a noisy ''chuck-chuck-chukar-chukar'' from which the name is derived.<ref name=fbi2>{{cite book|author=Baker ECS |year=1928| title= Fauna of British India. Birds. Volume 5 |edition=2nd|publisher=Taylor and Francis, London|pages=402–405|url=https://archive.org/stream/BakerFbiBirds5/BakerFBI5#page/n430/mode/1up/}}</ref> The [[Barbary partridge]] (''Alectoris barbara'') has a reddish-brown rather than black collar with a grey throat and face with a chestnut crown.<ref name=johnsgard/>

Other common names of this bird include '''chukker''' ('''chuker''' or '''chukor'''), '''Indian chukar''' and '''keklik'''.

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Chukar Patridge 2.jpg|thumb|Chukar Patridge from United Arab Emirates]]
This [[partridge]] has its native range in Asia, including [[Israel]], [[Jordan]], [[Palestine (region)|Palestine]], [[Lebanon]], [[Syria]], [[United Arab Emirates]], [[Saudi Arabia]], [[Oman]], [[Turkey]], [[Kurdistan]], [[Iran]], [[Afghanistan]], [[Tajikistan]], [[Pakistan]] and [[India]], along the inner ranges of the western [[Himalayas]] to [[Nepal]], as well as southeasternmost Europe in [[Bulgaria]] and [[Greece]].<ref name="iucn"/> Further west in southeastern Europe it is replaced by the [[rock partridge]], ''Alectoris graeca''. It barely ranges into Africa on the [[Sinai Peninsula]]. The habitat in the native range is rocky open hillsides with grass or scattered scrub or cultivation. In Israel and [[Jordan]] it is found at low altitudes, starting at {{Convert|400|m|abbr = on}} below sea level in the [[Dead Sea]] area, whereas in the more eastern areas it is mainly found at an altitude of {{Convert|2000|to|4000|m|ft|abbr = on}} except in Pakistan, where it occurs at {{Convert|600|m|abbr = on}}.<ref name=pcr/><ref name=whistler/> They are not found in areas of high humidity or rainfall.<ref name=baker_gbi/>

It has been [[introduced species|introduced]] widely as a game bird, and feral populations have become established in the United States ([[Rocky Mountains]], [[Great Basin]], [[Mojave Desert|high desert areas of California]]), [[Canada]], [[Chile]], [[Argentina]], [[New Zealand]] and [[Hawaii]].<ref>Long, John L. (1981). Introduced Birds of the World. Agricultural Protection Board of Western Australia, 21–493</ref> Initial introductions into the US were from the nominate populations collected from Afghanistan and Nepal.<ref>{{cite book|vauthors=Pyle RL, Pyle P|year=2009|title= The Birds of the Hawaiian Islands: Occurrence, History, Distribution, and Status|publisher= B.P. Bishop Museum, Honolulu, HI, U.S.A.|url=http://hbs.bishopmuseum.org/birds/rlp-monograph/pdfs/02-Galliformes-Procellariiformes/CHUK.pdf}}</ref> The birds are hunted across the dry, rocky [[Columbia Plateau|Columbia Basin]], especially in the vicinity of the [[Snake River]] in Washington and Oregon.<ref>{{cite news|title=Tough hunting: The challenge of chukar hunting brings its own rewards|author=Luke Thompson|newspaper=Yakima Herald-Republic|location=Yakima, Washington|date=October 23, 2019|url=https://www.yakimaherald.com/sports/outdoors/tough-hunting-the-challenge-of-chukar-hunting-brings-its-own-rewards/article_791169ed-afc0-557a-a4b0-f993096b4995.html}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Hunting chukar partridge|publisher=[[Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife]]|access-date=November 9, 2022|url=https://wdfw.wa.gov/hunting/requirements/upland-birds/chukar}}</ref> It has also been introduced to [[New South Wales]] in Australia but breeding populations have not persisted and are probably extinct.<ref>{{cite book|title=Systematics and Taxonomy of Australian Birds|url=https://archive.org/details/systematicstaxon00chri|url-access=limited|vauthors=Christidis L, Boles WE|year=2008|publisher=CSIRO|page=[https://archive.org/details/systematicstaxon00chri/page/n70 60]|isbn=978-0-643-06511-6}}</ref> A small population exists on [[Robben Island]] in South Africa since it was introduced there in 1964.<ref>[http://www.biodiversityexplorer.org/birds/phasianidae/alectoris_chukar.htm ''Alectoris chukar'' (Chukar partridge)] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180904170048/http://www.biodiversityexplorer.org/birds/phasianidae/alectoris_chukar.htm |date=2018-09-04 }}. Biodiversityexplorer.org. Retrieved 2011-11-28.</ref>

The chukar readily interbreeds with the [[red-legged partridge]] (''Alectoris rufa''), and the practice of breeding and releasing captive-bred hybrids has been banned in various countries including the [[United Kingdom]], as it is a threat to wild populations.<ref name="GWCT">{{cite web|url=http://www.gwct.org.uk/game/research/species/red-legged-partridge/|title=Red-legged partridge|access-date=2015-12-25|publisher=Game & Wildlife Conservation Trust}}</ref>

==Systematics and taxonomy==
[[File:Alectoris chukar 7 - Israel.jpg|thumb|''Alectoris chukar'']]

The chukar partridge is part of a confusing group of "red-legged partridges". Several plumage variations within the widespread distribution of the chukar partridge have been described and designated as subspecies. In the past the chukar group was included with the [[rock partridge]] (also known as the Greek partridge). The species from Turkey and farther east was subsequently separated from ''A. graeca'' of Greece and Bulgaria and western Europe.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Hartert |first=Ernst |author-link=Ernst Hartert |year=1925 |title=A new form of Chukar Partridge ''Alectoris graeca kleini'' subsp.nov. |url=https://archive.org/stream/novitateszoologi32lond#page/n163/mode/1up |journal=Novitates Zoologicae |volume=32 |page=137}}</ref><ref name=christensen>{{cite book|url=http://www.ndow.org/about/pubs/reports/1970_Chukar-Partridge_Bulletin.pdf|author=Christensen GC|year=1970|title=The Chukar Partridge. Biological Bulletin No. 4|publisher=Nevada Department of Wildlife|access-date=2010-07-21|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101011090518/http://www.ndow.org/about/pubs/reports/1970_Chukar-Partridge_Bulletin.pdf|archive-date=2010-10-11}}</ref>

===Subspecies===
The chukar has 14 recognized [[subspecies]]:<ref>{{Cite web |title=DOI – IOC World Bird List |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/ioc-lists/crossref/ |access-date=2023-03-18 |publisher=www.worldbirdnames.org |doi=10.14344/ioc.ml.13.1}}</ref>

* ''A. c. chukar'' (nominate species) <small>(J. E. Gray, 1830) - eastern [[Afghanistan]] to eastern [[Nepal]]</small>
* ''A. c. cypriotes'' <small>([[Ernst Hartert|Hartert]], 1917) - southeastern [[Bulgaria]] to southern [[Syria]], [[Crete]], [[Rhodes]] and [[Cyprus]]</small>
* ''A. c. dzungarica'' <small>(Sushkin, 1927) – northwestern [[Mongolia]] to [[Altai Mountains|Russian Altai]] and eastern [[Tibet]]</small>
* ''A. c. falki'' <small>([[Ernst Hartert|Hartert]], 1917) – north central [[Afghanistan]] to [[Pamir Mountains]] and western [[China]]</small>
* ''A. c. kleini'' <small>([[Ernst Hartert|Hartert]], 1925) – northern [[Greece]] through [[Bulgaria]] and north [[Turkey]] to the [[Caucasus]]</small>
* ''A. c. koroviakovi'' (<small>[[Nikolai Zarudny|Zarudny]], 1914) – eastern [[Iran]] to [[Pakistan]]</small>
* ''A. c. kurdestanica'' <small>([[Richard Meinertzhagen|Meinertzhagen]], 1923)</small> – <small>[[Caucasus Mountains]] to [[Iran]]</small>
* ''A. c. pallescens'' <small>([[Allan Octavian Hume|Hume]], 1873)  – northeastern [[Afghanistan]] to [[Ladakh]] and western [[Tibet]]</small>
* ''A. c. pallida'' <small>([[Allan Octavian Hume|Hume]], 1873) – northwestern [[China]]</small>
* ''A. c. potanini'' <small>(Sushkin, 1927) – western [[Mongolia]]</small>
* ''A. c. pubescens'' <small>([[Robert Swinhoe|R. Swinhoe]], 1871) – inner [[Mongolia]] to northwestern [[Sichuan]] and eastern [[Qinghai]]</small>
* ''A. c. sinaica'' <small>([[Charles Lucien Bonaparte|Bonaparte]], 1858) – northern [[Syrian Desert]] to [[Sinai Peninsula]]</small>
* ''A. c. subpallida'' <small>([[Nikolai Zarudny|Zarudny]], 1914) – [[Kyzylkum Desert|Kyzyl Kum]] and [[Karakum Desert|Kara Kum]] Mountains ([[Tajikistan]])</small>
* ''A. c. werae'' <small>([[Nikolai Zarudny|Zarudny]] and Loudon, 1904) – eastern [[Iraq]] and southwestern [[Iran]]</small>

==Population and status==
[[File:AntelopeIsland Chukar.JPG|thumb|right|Chukar partridge (right) in the [[Antelope Island State Park]], [[Utah]], US]]

This species is relatively unaffected by hunting or loss of habitat. Its numbers are largely affected by weather patterns during the breeding season. The release of captive stock in some parts of southern Europe can threaten native populations of rock partridge and red-legged partridge with which they may hybridize.<ref name=Barilani/><ref name=Duarte/>

British sportsmen in India considered the chukar as good sport although they were not considered to be particularly good in flavour. Their fast flight and ability to fly some distance after being shot made recovery of the birds difficult without retriever dogs.<ref name="humegame" /> During cold winters, when the higher areas are covered in snow, people in [[Kashmir]] have been known to use a technique to tire the birds out to catch them.<ref name="Ludlow" />

==Behaviour and ecology==
In the non-breeding season, chukar partridge are found in small coveys of 10 or more (up to 50) birds. In summer, chukars form pairs to breed. During this time, the cocks are very pugnacious in their calling and fighting.<ref name=whistler/><ref name=baker_gbi/><ref name=finn/><ref name=hbk/> During winter they descend into the valleys and feed in fields. They call frequently during the day and especially in the mornings and evenings. The call is loud and includes loud repeated ''chuck'' notes and sometimes duetting ''chuker'' notes. Several calls varying with context have been noted.<ref name=Stokes/> The most common call is a "rallying call" which when played back elicits a response from birds and has been used in surveys, although the method is not very reliable.<ref name=Williams/><ref name=Bohl/> When disturbed, it prefers to run rather than fly, but if necessary it flies a short distance often down a slope on rounded wings, calling immediately after alighting.<ref name=pcr/><ref name=humegame/><ref name=oates/> In Utah, birds were found to forage in an area of about {{Convert|2.6|km2|abbr = on}} and travel up to {{Convert|4.8|km|abbr = on}} to obtain water during the dry season. The home range was found to be even smaller in Idaho.<ref name=Walter/><ref name=Bump/><ref name=Phelps/>
The breeding season is summer. Males perform tidbitting displays, a form of [[courtship feeding]] where the male pecks at food and a female may visit to peck in response. The males may chase females with head lowered, wing lowered and neck fluffed. The male may also perform a high step stiff walk while making a special call. The female may then crouch in acceptance and the male mounts to copulate, while grasping the nape of the female. Males are monogamous.<ref name="christensen" /> The nest is a scantily lined ground scrape, though occasionally a compact pad is created with a depression in the centre. Generally, the nests are sheltered by ferns and small bushes, or placed in a dip or rocky hillside under an overhanging rock. About 7 to 14 eggs are laid.<ref name="baker_gbi" /><ref name="hbk" /><ref name="Hume" /> The eggs hatch in about 23–25 days. In captivity they can lay an egg each day during the breeding season if eggs are collected daily.<ref name="Woodard" /> Chicks join their parents in foraging and will soon join the chicks of other members of the covey.<ref name="johnsgard" />[[File:Chukar from Jebel Al Lawz, Saudi Arabia.jpg|left|thumb|Chukar from Jebel Al Lawz, Saudi Arabia]]As young chukars grow, and before flying for the first time, they utilize [[wing-assisted incline running]] as a transition to adult flight. This behaviour is found in several bird species, but has been extensively studied in chukar chicks, as a model to explain the [[origin of avian flight|evolution of avian flight]].<ref name="TobalskeDial2007">{{cite journal |author1=Tobalske, B. W.  |author2=Dial, K. P. |year=2007 |title=Aerodynamics of wing-assisted incline running in birds |journal=The Journal of Experimental Biology |volume=210 |pages=1742–1751|doi=10.1242/jeb.001701 |pmid=17488937 |issue=Pt 10|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name=Dialetal2006>{{cite journal|last1=Dial|first1=K. P.|last2=Randall|first2=R. J.|last3=Dial|first3=T. R. |title=What Use Is Half a Wing in the Ecology and Evolution of Birds? |journal=BioScience |volume=56 |issue=5 |pages=437–445 |doi=10.1641/0006-3568(2006)056[0437:WUIHAW]2.0.CO;2|year=2006|url=http://scholarworks.umt.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1043&context=biosci_pubs|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="Dial2003WAIR">{{cite journal |author=Dial, K.P. |year=2003 |title=Wing-Assisted Incline Running and the Evolution of Flight |journal=Science |volume=299 |issue=5605 |pages=402–404 |doi=10.1126/science.1078237 |url=http://dbs.umt.edu/flightlab/pdf/WAIR.SCIENCE.pdf | pmid=12532020 |bibcode = 2003Sci...299..402D |s2cid=40712093 }}</ref><ref name="BundleDial2003MechanicsOfWAIR">{{cite journal |year=2003 |title=Mechanics of wing-assisted incline running (WAIR) |journal=The Journal of Experimental Biology |volume=206 |pages=4553–4564 |url=http://dbs.umt.edu/flightlab/pdf/bundle%20and%20dial%20JEB%202003.pdf |author1=Bundle, M.W.  |author2=Dial, K.P. |doi=10.1242/jeb.00673 | pmid=14610039 |issue=Pt 24 |s2cid=6323207 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

[[File:FarahChukar.png|thumb|upright|150px|A chukar in a 17th-century Persian encyclopedia]]

Chukar will take a wide variety of seeds and some [[insect]]s as food. It also ingests grit.<ref name=oates /> In Kashmir, the seeds of a species of ''[[Eragrostis]]'' was particularly dominant in their diet<ref name=Oakleaf/> while those in the US favoured ''[[Bromus tectorum]]''.<ref name=johnsgard/> Birds feeding on succulent vegetation make up for their water needs but visit open water in summer.<ref name=Degen/>

Chukar roost on rocky slopes or under shrubs. In the winter, birds in the US selected protected niches or caves. A group may roost in a tight circle with their heads pointed outwards to conserve heat and keep a look out for predators.<ref name=johnsgard/>

Chukar are sometimes preyed on by [[golden eagle]]s.<ref name="bbb">{{cite journal |author=Ticehurst |first=Claud B. |author-link=Claud B. Ticehurst |year=1927 |title=The Birds of British Baluchistan. Part 3 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47859292 |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=32 |issue=1 |pages=64–97}}</ref>

Birds in captivity can die from ''[[Mycoplasma]]'' infection and outbreaks of other diseases such as [[erysipelas]].<ref name=Lateef/><ref name=Pettit/><ref name=Dubey/>

==In culture==
The name is [[onomatopoeic]] and mentions of ''chakor'' in Sanskrit, from northern Indian date back to the ''[[Markandeya Purana]]'' (c. 250–500 AD).<ref>{{Cite book |last=Dowson |first=John |author-link=John Dowson |url=https://archive.org/stream/aclassicaldicti00dowsgoog#page/n91/mode/1up/ |title=A Classical Dictionary of Hindu Mythology and Religion, Geography, History and Literature |publisher=Trübner & Co. |year=1879 |series=Trübner's Oriental Series VI |place=London |page=[https://archive.org/details/aclassicaldicti00dowsgoog/page/n90/mode/1up 65]}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=Pargiter |first=Frederick Eden |author-link=F. E. Pargiter |url=https://archive.org/stream/cu31924022991974#page/n73/mode/1up/ |title=The Markandeya Purana. Translated with Notes |publisher=The Asiatic Society |year=1904 |place=Calcutta |page=28}}</ref> In [[North India]]n and [[Pakistani]] culture, as well as in [[Hindu mythology]], the chukar (referred as Chakor) sometimes symbolizes intense, and often unrequited, love.<ref name="temple1884" /><ref name="jasb1881">{{cite journal| journal=Journal of the Asiatic Society of Bengal |volume=55 |  year=1881 |title=Translation of the Songs of Harkh Na'Th| publisher=Asiatic Society of Bengal |page=121| url=https://books.google.com/books?id=crwIAAAAQAAJ | quote=When I beheld thy face mournful, lady, I wandered restlessly o'er the world, Thy face is like the moon, and my heart like the chakor }}</ref> It is said to be in love with the [[moon]] and gazes at it constantly.<ref name="balfour1871">{{Cite book |author=Balfour |first=Edward |author-link=Edward Balfour |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jmoIAAAAQAAJ |title=Cyclopædia of India and of eastern and southern Asia, commercial, industrial and scientific: products of the mineral, vegetable and animal kingdoms, useful arts and manufactures |publisher=Scottish & Adelphi Presses |year=1871 |quote=The birds are said by the natives to be enamoured of the moon and, at full moon, to eat fire}}</ref> Because of their pugnacious behaviour during the breeding season they are kept in some areas as fighting birds.<ref name=baker_gbi/><ref name=finn/>

zh.extract:
石鸡（学名：Alectoris chukar）为雉科石鸡属的鸟类，俗名朵拉鸡、红腿鸡、嘎嘎鸡、鷓鴣。分布于欧洲、西伯利亚、阿富汗、伊拉克、伊朗、克什米尔以及中国大陆的新疆、青海、甘肃、经华北到东北的西南部等地，多生活于低山干燥山谷以及丘陵的岩坡和砂坡。该物种的模式产地在希腊。


亚种
石鸡北疆亚种（学名：Alectoris chukar dzungarica）。分布于蒙古以及中国新疆等地。该物种的模式产地在中国塔尔巴哈台蒙拉克山。
石鸡新疆亚种（学名：Alectoris chukar falki）。分布于阿富汗、帕米尔高原北部、哈萨克斯坦以及中国新疆等地。该物种的模式产地在吉尔吉斯斯坦伊塞克湖。
石鸡内蒙亚种（学名：Alectoris chukar ordoscensis）。
石鸡疆西亚种（学名：Alectoris chukar pallescens）。分布于阿富汗、帕米尔高原、克什米尔以及中国新疆等地。该物种的模式产地在库鲁山。
石鸡南疆亚种（学名：Alectoris chukar pallida）。分布于中国新疆、青海等地。该物种的模式产地在新疆葉爾羌。
石鸡贺兰山亚种（学名：Alectoris chukar potanini）。分布于蒙古以及中国甘肃、新疆等地。该物种的模式产地在贺兰山。
石鸡华北亚种（学名：Alectoris chukar pubescens）。分布中国于东北、内蒙古、河北、河南、山东、山西、江苏、陕西、甘肃等地。该物种的模式产地在华北至长江上游。


保护
本种于2023年被收录入《有重要生态、科学、社会价值的陆生野生动物名录》。
